nativeRange.habitat: Reef slopes and rubble zones with moderate-to-strong tidal currents; occasionally in shadier, lower-light nearshore areas

decorPreferences: open placement with spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiosis. Supplemental target feeding with phytoplankton or coral-specific micro-foods is generally considered beneficial but not required for survival; feeding every one to two weeks has been reported to support growth.

goodWith: other soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: slow-growing stony corals without adequate spacing

imageCandidates.notes: Emőke Dénes; 5184x3456. Whole tree-like colony under blue actinic reef lighting — the look hobbyists buy; central trunk with radiating polyp-covered branches clearly visible (soft foreground trunk only).
PilarMeca; 1809x2573. Tack-sharp textbook Kenya Tree: cream-tan trunk and branches with fine cauliflower polyp tips, whole colony in frame (daylight).
Emőke Dénes; 5184x3456. Sharp actinic close view of polyp-studded branches, strong reef-tank colour.
User:Haplochromis; 628x942. Classic tan-grey aquarium colony showing branching tree form and cream polyp tips (lower resolution).
PilarMeca; 3072x2048. Pink/mauve whole colony drooping over rock — colour variety within the genus.
Peter Southwood; 3648x2736. Wild in-situ reef colony (cauliflower soft coral) among reef life — shows natural habitat and a white/cream form.
User:Haplochromis; 2400x1600. Macro of cauliflower polyp heads with pink tips — diagnostic close-up detail.
PilarMeca; 3072x2048. Branch close-up with polyps extended, showing the fine polyp texture.

summary: The Kenya Tree Coral is a soft coral prized for its tree-like branching form and tolerant, beginner-friendly care. Capnella is widely regarded as one of the most vigorously spreading corals in reef aquaria, regularly shedding branches that reattach and grow into new colonies — a tendency that rewards regular pruning.
careNotes:
Growth form and appearance: Capnella forms an upright, tree-like colony with a central trunk and radiating branches. Colony color ranges from tan-grey to brown, often with lighter or cream-colored polyp tips. Coloration has been reported to lighten under higher light intensities.

Lighting: Generally advised to provide 75–150 PAR; this coral tends to tolerate a wide lighting range. Excessively bright, direct high-intensity light has been known to cause polyp retraction and shriveling. Acclimate new specimens gradually to the intended light level.

Flow: Moderate, indirect flow is generally recommended. In the wild, Capnella occupies reef slopes exposed to tidal currents, but strong laminar flow directed at the colony has been known to cause persistent polyp closure. Gentle to moderate alternating flow is preferred.

Spreading tendency (important): Capnella is widely considered one of the fastest-spreading corals in reef aquaria. Branches regularly swell at their base and detach naturally (a form of budding/fission), dropping to the substrate where they attach within 24–48 hours. Unmanaged colonies have been reported to cover substantial areas of rockwork. Regular fragging and removal of detached babies is strongly advised in mixed-coral tanks.

Fragging: Extremely easy — one of the simplest corals to propagate. Pinching off or cutting a branch with sharp scissors or a razor blade and placing it on rubble or a frag plug is generally sufficient; pieces tend to attach within a few days without adhesive. Many keepers recommend simply pinching branches by hand.

Allelopathy: Capnella has been reported to release terpene-class chemical compounds, similar to those produced by Sarcophyton (toadstool) corals. These substances may inhibit growth of nearby corals by affecting cellular division. Maintaining a clear space of at least 5–10 cm around the colony and running activated carbon in the sump is generally advisable, especially in tanks with sensitive SPS corals.

Capnella is widely considered one of the most beginner-friendly soft corals available. It tends to tolerate water quality fluctuations that would stress more demanding corals and generally does not require pristine, ultra-low-nutrient conditions. For most keepers, managing its vigorous spread proves to be the greater challenge rather than keeping it alive.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction is not practiced in the hobby. Capnella propagates in captivity almost exclusively by vegetative fragmentation. The coral naturally sheds small branches (swellings form at branch bases, then detach via fission), which settle and re-root within a day or two. Intentional fragging by pinching or cutting branches is extremely easy and is one of the most beginner-accessible coral fragging techniques; no specialized equipment is required. Frags are commonly shared at reef clubs and swaps.

primary.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder – Kenya Tree Coral Care
primary.notes: Primary source; provided water parameters, PAR, flow, placement, tank size, care level, feeding, fragging, spreading, and allelopathy details.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog – Kenya Tree Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (22.7–27.7°C), pH, salinity (1.025), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), calcium (350–450 ppm), tank size (30 gal / 114 L for mixed), spreading tendency, and allelopathy. Agreed on beginner care level. Temperature range aligned; calcium lower bound (350) noted but 380 adopted as conservative average with primary.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Kenya Tree Coral Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–79°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.023–1.025), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), flow (moderate), care level (easy), feeding, fragging, spreading, and allelopathy. Narrower temperature upper bound (26°C) noted; averaged with other sources for final 28°C max.

additional.name: Reef Chasers – Kenya Tree Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked PAR (100–150), flow (low-moderate), calcium (400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), care level (beginner). Wider upper temperature (84°F / 28.9°C) noted and incorporated into max. Confirms allelopathy chemical release and activated carbon recommendation.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Capnella (taxonomy and biology)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Nephtheidae, Order: Malacalcyonacea), native range (Indo-Pacific coast, reef slopes), growth form description, and asexual reproduction mechanism. Not used for husbandry parameters.
